text: Hello friends, I am Dr. Vinod Chaudhary. Welcome to my channel of laparoscopic videos. Today we are going to discuss about the case. In the previous two LSCF patients, she was diagnosed as an ectopic pregnancy.

She went home on that day and returned on the third day with severe pain in abdomen and diagnosed with seven weeks ruptured tubal ectopic pregnancy and which was managed laparoscopically. This was a little difficult case. In this case, we have found seabed adhesions. The other side tube is adherent with the omentum and the lateral pelvic wall and there was a gross hemoperitoneum. Up to the one point five liter of blood is there in the cavity.
Kindly do subscribe the channel and press the bell icon to get notified for my upcoming videos on the laparoscopy.

The surgeon demonstrates a direct trocar entry technique, making a 10mm incision on the suprapubic area, 2 centimeters above the umbilicus. The abdominal wall is lifted, and the trocar is inserted at a 90-degree angle. Carbon dioxide is connected for insufflation, and the telescope is introduced to visualize the peritoneal cavity, revealing the omentum positioned towards the head.

The surgeon inserts a 5mm trocar on the left side, positioned two centimeters above and lateral to the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), under direct vision. The external video shows the trocar entry, with caution advised due to widespread omental adhesions, requiring careful navigation to find a suitable entry space.

The surgeon inserts the second trocar, noting that the proximity of the primary port causes some visual obstruction. Omental adhesions are visible, contributing to the vision difficulties. The surgeon prepares to use a bipolar cutter for the procedure.

The surgeon removes adhesions, staying close to the intra-abdominal wall. The bipolar cutter is used to coagulate any bleeders on the resected omentum. Thin, flimsy adhesions are visible, with the omentum adherent to the anterior abdominal wall, lateral pelvic wall, fallopian tube, and round ligament. No bowel content is present, confirming the absence of a hernia.

The surgeon carefully dissects the adhesion from the anterior abdominal wall, moving laterally to separate the adherent bladder and ensure the functionality of the fallopian tube. The adhesion is dense, likely due to severe PID, and the surgeon uses precise techniques to separate the structures.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector in the left hand to apply downward traction while dissecting the omentum with a bipolar cutter in the right hand, set at forty watts. The dissection is performed laterally to avoid injuring the fallopian tube on the opposite side. Gas insufflation into the peritoneum aids in separating small parts of the omentum, ensuring proper coagulation and minimizing bleeding.

The surgeon uses electrocautery set at forty watts to coagulate and cut the omentum, ensuring no bleeding occurs. The omentum is mainly separated, and the surgeon proceeds to separate small adhesions from the posterior wall and fundus, coagulating visible blood vessels connecting them.

The surgeon identifies a small point of bleeding and coagulates it. Attention is then directed towards separating the adhesions affecting the fallopian tube, which is kinked and distorted. The surgeon carefully dissects the flimsy adhesions to restore the anatomy, ensuring minimal thermal damage by remaining lateral. The fimbria, adherent to the tube, are also separated to enable proper ovum pickup, mobilizing the tube to achieve correct anatomical positioning.

The surgeon encounters bladder fibers and peritoneum adherent to the round ligament and fundus. The detector is repositioned to the left hand, applying medial traction to the tube without direct manipulation, aiming to remove kinking and free the cymbria.

The surgeon carefully removes the remaining adhesions from the tube, using gradual and precise movements to free it completely. The adhesions appear flimsy but are challenging to dissect, and their removal is crucial to restore proper anatomical function and prevent infertility. The tube is held securely to ensure complete separation before addressing the ectopic area.

The surgeon addresses the ectopic pregnancy by performing a partial salpingectomy on the ruptured tube. The decidual tissue has been removed, and the tube is extracted through a 5mm trocar without suturing, due to the risk of ectopic recurrence.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to coagulate a small vessel, possibly a salping gel vessel or an anastomosis between the ovarian and uterine vessels. The surgical field is clear with no bleeding. The surgeon then focuses on suctioning, removing blood clots from the cavity, which appear to be from a fresh rupture. Adhesions between the bladder and uterus are visible, likely due to previous surgeries. Suctioning begins in the pouch of Douglas (POD) to clear the area and manage clots.

The surgeon focuses on suctioning clots from the pelvic cavity using normal saline, without heparin. Clots are pushed into the pouch of Douglas (POD) for easier removal. The operating table is adjusted to a head-up position, causing accumulated blood from the upper abdomen to flow downward. The blood, previously collected in the upper quadrants during Trendelenburg positioning, is now visible around the liver and stomach. The anterior abdominal wall is stained with blood, indicating a raw seroperitoneum. Approximately 1.5 liters of blood is measured, and suctioning is performed around the liver.

The surgeon uses two ports to perform suctioning of collected blood between the liver and costal margins, ensuring the intestine and mesentery are not disturbed. The operating table is straightened, revealing collected fluid and blood in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs final irrigation and suctioning, clearing the surgical cavity of blood and fluid. The cavity appears clear, with only some fluid remaining, which will be absorbed and help prevent adhesion formation. A biograsper is used to retract the omentum and bowel during the suctioning process.
